explanation_md: When `printB()` is called, it first calls `printA()` which prints `A`, then prints `B`. Back in `main`, the second call to `printA()` prints `A` again. Execution follows each call to its body, then returns to the next statement after the call site.

prompt_md: In C++, what happens if you write `sayHello;` (without parentheses) instead of `sayHello();` inside `main`?

options.a: It calls the function and runs its body.
options.b: It causes a compile error every time.
options.c: It compiles but the function body does not run.
options.d: It prints the function's name to the screen.

explanation_md: Without parentheses, `sayHello` is a reference to the function object, not a call. On many compilers this produces at most a warning but does compile. The body never executes, so no output is produced. The parentheses `()` are what trigger execution.
`main` runs sequentially: it prints `start`, then calls `line` which prints `---`, then prints `end`. After `line()` returns, execution continues at the statement immediately after the call site.

options.b: The compiler reads top-to-bottom and has not seen `greet` before the call.
options.c: You cannot call a `void` function from `main`.

explanation_md: The compiler processes a file from top to bottom. When it encounters `greet()` inside `main`, the function definition for `greet` has not appeared yet, so the compiler reports an undeclared identifier error. Moving the definition above `main` (or adding a forward declaration) fixes it.
